metadata.from: Bob (Robert Hanifen) — signed 'Love Your Brother Bob'
metadata.to: Jim & Helen & Jimmy III & Rosemary — Jim is likely Bob's brother James Edward Hanifen Jr., with his wife Helen and children Jimmy III and Rosemary
metadata.envelope_notes: No envelope visible. Letter written on novelty stationery featuring a red martini glass graphic with the caption 'High Again Jan'
metadata.mood_tone: Casual, friendly, and mildly self-deprecating; upbeat despite financial constraints; humor about potentially changing his mind on sending the check
metadata.legibility_notes: Several words are difficult to read due to cursive style and ink flow; some amounts partially unclear (e.g., '$20.00' for tires, '$35.00' raise); the word 'lot' appears written as 'lot' with a strikethrough and rewrite; 'two' is written and then crossed out to correct; income tax amount reads as '137.49' with '.49' kept for necessity

pages.transcription:
March 26, 1952

Dear Jim & Helen & Jimmy III
& Rosemary

Well here I am again without too much news but with a lot of money for you. I am hopping [sic] to pay you all of the balance inside of two months. I got two tires last & tubes for $20.00 Goodyear. I know a guy who works for them. It is hell not being able to get everything wholesale like I used to. My Income Tax Ret. was 137.49. I am keeping the .49 for necessity. I haven't heard from John in some time. I sent some pictures of the new cars awhile ago. Haven't heard from anyone to far as that. I know the reason why only hoped you'd understand.

I am still with the The Texas Co. pencil pushing for a living haven't got a raise as yet. Jan got another job. It pays the same as mine with a $35.00 raise in 3 months. Well I guess I had better

send you the check before I change my mind.

Love Your Brother
Bob

full_transcription:
March 26, 1952

Dear Jim & Helen & Jimmy III
& Rosemary

Well here I am again without too much news but with a lot of money for you. I am hopping [sic] to pay you all of the balance inside of two months. I got two tires last & tubes for $20.00 Goodyear. I know a guy who works for them. It is hell not being able to get everything wholesale like I used to. My Income Tax Ret. was 137.49. I am keeping the .49 for necessity. I haven't heard from John in some time. I sent some pictures of the new cars awhile ago. Haven't heard from anyone to far as that. I know the reason why only hoped you'd understand.

I am still with the The Texas Co. pencil pushing for a living haven't got a raise as yet. Jan got another job. It pays the same as mine with a $35.00 raise in 3 months. Well I guess I had better send you the check before I change my mind.

Love Your Brother
Bob
summary: In this March 1952 letter, Bob writes to his brother Jim and family promising to repay a debt, noting he hopes to pay the full balance within two months. He shares updates about purchasing Goodyear tires at a discount through a contact, receiving a $137.49 income tax refund (keeping only the $0.49 for himself), and continuing his job at The Texas Company. He humorously closes by saying he'd better send the check before he changes his mind.
